items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: Who proposed the Multi-Store Model in 1968?
items.why: It's Atkinson & Shiffrin. “Baddeley & Hitch” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Iconic memory has a duration of approximately how long?
items.why: It's 250 ms. “2 seconds” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Multi-Store Model published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1968. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
